flashcards.front: What is a Binary Search Tree (BST)?
flashcards.back: A binary tree where each node has at most two children, and the left subtree contains only nodes with values less than the parent, while the right subtree contains only nodes with values greater than the parent.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of search operation in a balanced BST?
flashcards.back: O(log n) - we eliminate half of the remaining nodes at each step by comparing the target with the current node's value.

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case time complexity of BST search?
flashcards.back: O(n) - occurs when the BST is skewed (essentially becomes a linked list), requiring traversal of all nodes.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of insertion in a BST?
flashcards.back: O(log n) average case for balanced BST, O(n) worst case for skewed tree. We find the appropriate leaf position and insert the new node.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of deletion in a BST?
flashcards.back: O(log n) average case. Three cases: leaf node (simple), one child (connect to parent), two children (replace with inorder successor/predecessor).

flashcards.front: What is the height of a BST?
flashcards.back: The height is the number of edges on the longest path from the root to a leaf node. Empty tree has height -1, single node has height 0.

flashcards.front: What does inorder traversal of a BST produce?
flashcards.back: Inorder traversal produces nodes in sorted (ascending) order. For BST, it visits left subtree, root, then right subtree.

flashcards.front: How do you find the minimum value in a BST?
flashcards.back: Start from root and keep moving to left child until NULL is reached. Time complexity: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case. The minimum is the leftmost node.

flashcards.front: How do you find the maximum value in a BST?
flashcards.back: Start from root and keep moving to right child until NULL is reached. Time complexity: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case. The maximum is the rightmost node.
A skewed BST is a tree where each node has only one child, making it essentially a linear structure like a linked list. This results in O(n) time complexity for all operations.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of BST operations (recursive)?
flashcards.back: O(h) where h is the height of the tree, representing the recursion stack depth. O(log n) for balanced, O(n) for skewed.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between BST and Binary Tree?
flashcards.back: Binary tree is any tree where each node has max 2 children. BST is a binary tree with additional property: left < root < right for all nodes.

flashcards.front: What is an inorder successor in BST?
flashcards.back: The inorder successor is the smallest node in the right subtree of a given node (or the leftmost node in the right subtree). Used in deletion of node with two children.

flashcards.front: What is the preorder traversal order in BST?
flashcards.back: Root → Left Subtree → Right Subtree. Useful for creating a copy of tree or getting prefix expression.

flashcards.front: Why is BST preferred over sorted array for search?
flashcards.back: BST provides O(log n) search like sorted array but with O(log n) insertion and deletion (vs O(n) for array), making it more efficient for dynamic data.
